caption: The four math areas used throughout ML: linear algebra, statistics, information theory, and optimization
Learning rate too high (oscillates), just right (converges), too low (stalls) — with loss surface
Common discrete distributions (Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson) and continuous distributions (Gaussian, Exponential, Beta)
For continuous variables, probability equals the area under the curve between two points — not a point value
The Gaussian 68-95-99.7 rule: almost all data falls within 3 standard deviations of the mean
Exponential distribution: models waiting time. Memoryless property means past wait doesn't affect future expectation
How to choose a distribution: discrete vs continuous, then match to your data pattern
Skewness (left/symmetric/right) and kurtosis (light/normal/heavy tails) with Python-style plots
NumPy vs Pandas: ndarray for math operations, DataFrame for labeled tabular wrangling — two complementary tools
EDA workflow: inspect structure → check quality → explore distributions → plan transformations → implement leakage-safe
Algorithm selection guide: pick by output type (regression/classification/dim-reduction), then by data shape and interpretability needs
Sigmoid function maps any real-valued score to a probability between 0 and 1 for binary classification
K-Nearest Neighbors: k=1 uses nearest point only (sensitive), k=5 votes across 5 neighbors (more stable)
Early stopping: training loss keeps falling but validation loss bottoms out — stop at the minimum to prevent overfitting
Residual diagnostic plots: good residuals (random, centred), heteroskedasticity (fan pattern), and nonlinear pattern — each signals a different model problem
SVM kernel trick: circular XOR data is not linearly separable in 2D, but the RBF kernel maps it to 3D where a flat hyperplane separates classes
Gradient boosting: each successive tree fits the residuals of the previous ensemble, progressively improving the fit
Decision tree: recursive splits on Gini impurity with fraud detection example — internal nodes test features, leaves predict class
Random Forest: independent trees trained on bootstrap samples vote to produce a final majority-vote prediction
Partial Dependence Plot (PDP): average effect of transaction amount on predicted fraud risk, marginalizing all other features
Individual Conditional Expectation (ICE) lines per observation vs the average Partial Dependence Plot (PDP) overlay
SHAP waterfall: each feature's additive contribution to a single prediction, from base value to final output
SHAP beeswarm summary: distribution of feature contributions across all predictions — feature importance + direction
Three-way split: train to learn weights, validation to tune decisions, test for one honest final evaluation
Data leakage: fitting the scaler on all data before splitting lets the model see future information — always fit on train only
Confusion matrix with derived metrics: Precision (TP/(TP+FP)), Recall (TP/(TP+FN)), and Specificity (TN/(TN+FP))
Precision-Recall-F1 tradeoff: raising the classification threshold increases precision but reduces recall
Unsupervised learning: three families — clustering (group), dimensionality reduction (compress), anomaly detection (flag unusual)
K-means clustering: unlabeled points are assigned to the nearest centroid (marked with X) and color-coded by cluster
Choosing k: inertia elbow method (drop flattens) and silhouette score peak both point to k=3
PCA scree plot (per-component variance) and cumulative explained variance curve — use the knee to pick how many components to keep
Isolation Forest: normal points require many random splits to isolate (long path); anomalies are isolated with just a few splits (short path)
Full unsupervised learning selection guide: clustering, dimensionality reduction, anomaly detection, and representation learning pathways
Recommendation paradigms: collaborative filtering (user behavior), content-based (item attributes), hybrid, and popularity baseline
Neural network training loop: forward pass → compute loss → backpropagation → optimizer updates weights → repeat
Text representation evolution: Bag-of-Words/TF-IDF → Word2Vec/GloVe static embeddings → BERT/LLM contextual representations
Reinforcement learning loop: agent takes an action, environment returns next state and reward, policy improves over time
Time series forecasting pipeline: past values + exogenous variables → model → future predictions
Decomposing a time series into trend (long-run direction), seasonality (periodic pattern), and residual (unexplained noise)
Walk-forward (expanding window) validation: train grows each fold, test is always the next unseen period
Data flow from OLTP (transactional, row-optimized) to OLAP (analytical, column-optimized) systems
ETL (transform before loading) vs ELT (load raw, transform inside warehouse) — when to use each
End-to-end ML pipeline: data ingestion → feature engineering → training → evaluation → serving → monitoring
SageMaker pipeline as a directed acyclic graph: each step (processing, training, evaluation, registration) is a node
Responsible AI is a cross-cutting constraint at every lifecycle stage: data, modeling, evaluation, deployment, and monitoring
Global explainability (what features matter overall) vs local explainability (why this specific prediction)
LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations): fit a simple linear surrogate around one prediction to explain it
Five-step ML system design framework: problem → data → features+model → evaluation → serving+monitoring
